categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's risk profile across five universal lenses: overall volatility (which also carries the worst-drawdown and capture / downside-protection context), risk-adjusted return (which also folds in the drawdown-vs-benchmark check), risk handling vs the fund's category peers, macro environment risk (economy, industry cycle, interest rates, currency), and structural NAV-erosion risk with a strategy-validation test. Pass/Fail is always mandate-based: a high-beta thematic fund is expected to swing, a low-vol fund is expected to be calm, a covered-call fund is expected to trade upside for downside protection, a managed-futures fund is expected to decorrelate from equities — each is judged against its own job, not against a broad-equity benchmark. Each factor's `factorAnalysisDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and the mandate-based Pass/Fail rule; `groupInstructions` carries the 1-2 line group-specific perspective (typical beta / standard-deviation range, comparison index, drawdown norm, peer-comparison guardrails, the macro forces that matter for that group, and whether NAV-erosion is structurally a thing). When the two appear to conflict, the group instructions win.

factors.factorDescription: Measures how much the ETF's price moves day-to-day and month-to-month, using beta (sensitivity vs the broad market — typically the S&P 500 for equity / multi-asset funds) and standard deviation (size of typical return swings). The reading must match the fund's mandate: a 'low-volatility' equity fund with beta near 1.0 is failing at its stated job; a 3x leveraged fund SHOULD have beta near 3.0 on the underlying; a multi-sector credit fund has near-zero equity beta by design and should be judged via standard deviation. This factor also carries short context on the worst calendar-year drawdown (the bumpiness the investor would actually feel) and on basic upside / downside capture vs the group benchmark (does the volatility you take show up as one-sided pain or symmetric variation around the index). For young funds (under 2 years), beta is noisy — flag the short history and lean on the category comparison. Pass when the volatility profile, worst-year, and capture pattern all fit the fund's mandate and category norm. Fail when volatility is materially mismatched to mandate, the worst-year is materially deeper than category peers in the same stress window, or downside capture is near 100% on a fund that is sold as defensive / income / hedged.
factors.factorMetrics: beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, atr, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, drawdown, drawdownDates, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent, captureRatios, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Beta near 1.0 vs the S&P 500 is the baseline for US large blend / total market; style tilts should show the matching deviation (low-vol below 0.8, growth above 1.0 in growth cycles, dividend / value typically 0.85-1.0); foreign and global funds judged vs MSCI EAFE / MSCI ACWI. Standard deviation typical 12-18% annualized; worst-year -20% to -35% (2008, 2020, 2022 are the reference shocks). Capture should sit near 100% on both sides for passive — material deviation = factor tilt or tracking issue.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Beta vs S&P 500 typically runs 1.0-1.4 for cyclicals and tech, below 1.0 for defensives (consumer staples, utilities, healthcare); standard deviation 18-30%. Worst single year often -40% to -60% in a bad sector window (tech 2022, energy 2020, biotech 2022); single-country EM can drop -50%+. Capture pattern is often asymmetric — high upside in the sector's good window, deep downside in its bad — note this.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Beta should sit close to the stated daily-leverage multiple on the underlying (3x equity fund → beta near 3.0 against the underlying; -2x inverse → beta near -2.0). Standard deviation routinely 30-100%+. Worst-year -70% to -90% in 3x funds during adverse years; capture mechanically follows the leverage. The retail message: high volatility is BY DESIGN — judge whether daily-tracking quality holds, not whether the volatility number is large.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Equity-S&P beta is the right correlation read (gold near zero is the diversification claim; BTC has run 0.6-0.8 recently). Standard deviation tells the absolute volatility story — gold 12-18% annualized, broad commodities 15-25%, crypto frequently above 60%. Gold has had multi-year drawdowns near -45%; broad commodities -20-30%; BTC repeatedly -75% to -83%. A gold fund showing S&P-like beta in an equity drawdown has lost its diversification value.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Group dispersion is wide — covered-call funds typically run 50-70% of S&P vol; market-neutral and managed-futures often 20-40%; long-short between. Capture pattern: covered-call funds should run roughly 60-80% upside and 50-70% downside capture vs the underlying; if upside is near 100%, the options program is not binding; if downside is near 100%, the protection it charges for is not showing up. Worst-year vs underlying — e.g. JEPI -13% vs S&P -25% in 2022 = mandate delivered.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Standard deviation 4-8% conservative, 8-12% moderate, 12-16% aggressive; target-date glide paths show declining vol over time. Beta vs S&P proportional to equity weight (~0.6 moderate, ~0.85 aggressive). Worst-year vs 100% equity should be materially smaller (a 60/40 that lost -20% when stocks lost -20% failed at its core mandate; -12% to -14% would be in line). Capture should show smoothing — meaningfully under 100% on the downside.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Equity-market beta is misleading — judge via standard deviation. Ultrashort 1-3% stdev; intermediate core 4-7%; long government / muni 10-15%; money market essentially zero. Worst calendar year (2022 rate-shock test): long IG / muni -25% to -31%; intermediate core ~-13%; ultrashort ~-3%; MMF flat. Compare worst year to a duration-matched Treasury index — in line is Pass.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Equity-beta matters here in a way it does not for IG — HY corporate 0.4-0.6, bank loans 0.2-0.4, preferred 0.5-0.7, convertibles often 0.6+ (more equity than bond). Standard deviation 6-12% typical. Worst-year context: 2020 COVID hit HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%, EM debt -15%, preferreds -15-25%; 2022 hit longer-duration credit harder; 2008 GFC was the deepest credit stress (HYG -22%).

factors.factorTitle: Are You Paid Fairly for the Risk
factors.factorDescription: Measures how much return investors earned per unit of risk taken, using Sharpe (excess return ÷ total volatility) and Sortino (excess return ÷ downside volatility). The threshold is category-specific. This factor also folds in the downside-protection check: in actual stress windows (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2008 GFC where applicable), did the fund's drawdown match what its Sharpe / strategy promised? A defensive / allocation / income / diversifier fund with a respectable Sharpe but a near-100% downside-capture ratio is failing the practical risk-adjusted test. For active funds Sharpe is the honest test of whether manager picks added real risk-adjusted value; for passive funds Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was an efficient exposure. For young funds (under 3 years) Sharpe is unreliable and the short history must be stated. Pass when Sharpe is at or above the category median over the longest available multi-year window, Sortino is consistent with Sharpe (no hidden downside story), and the actual stress-window drawdown ratio matches what the mandate promises. Fail when Sharpe materially trails category median without a mandate-based explanation, OR Sortino is materially weaker than Sharpe (hidden downside), OR a fund sold as defensive shows no meaningful drawdown protection in the latest stress window.
factors.factorMetrics: sharpe, sortino, portfolioRiskScore, morAnalyzerRiskReturn, captureRatios, drawdown, riskVsCategory, returnVsCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Sharpe above 0.5 is decent and above 1.0 is very good over a multi-year window. Compare to the S&P 500's Sharpe in the same window — a passive Large Blend should be within tracking distance; an active or tilt fund should show whether the tilt paid for the extra risk it took. Drawdown ratio close to 1.0 vs the index is expected for passive — that is the asset class, not a Fail.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector Sharpe is more volatile than broad-equity Sharpe — read multi-year context (tech and discretionary lead in growth cycles, defensives in late-cycle). Do not Fail a sector fund for the wrong-half-of-cycle Sharpe; the honest test is whether the Sharpe is at or above the sector-peer median over a multi-year window. Stress drawdown vs the sector index, not the S&P 500.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Multi-year Sharpe is essentially meaningless here — daily-reset decay destroys the risk/return relationship. Judge on short-horizon suitability and on whether the realized returns track the leverage multiple of the underlying with reasonable fidelity, not on the long-window Sharpe number. Drawdown is mechanically the leverage factor times the underlying's drawdown minus reset slippage — quote both numbers side by side.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Sharpe varies enormously by window (BTC has swung from very high to deeply negative inside two years; gold ranges 0.2 to 0.8 over multi-year windows). Read multi-year context and judge against the asset's own history, not against equity Sharpe. The honest drawdown test: gold should show positive or near-flat returns in equity drawdowns (the diversification claim) — a gold fund that fell with equities in 2022 has lost its hedging case.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Derivative-income and hedge-style funds can show low or even negative Sharpe during equity bull markets yet still deliver on a hedging mandate — judge against category peers, not against broad equity. The drawdown test is the honest one: covered-call funds should show meaningfully lower drawdown than the underlying (JEPI -13% vs S&P -25% in 2022 = mandate met). Market-neutral and managed-futures should be roughly flat or positive in equity bear years; if they were not, the mandate is not being delivered.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Sharpe of 0.5-1.0 is typical for allocation and target-date funds — a moderate-allocation fund matching 60/40 returns with lower vol is doing its job even if absolute return lags pure equity. Compare to a passive 60/40 same-period Sharpe AND check the drawdown ratio vs 100% equity in 2022 / 2020 (moderate should land at roughly 60-70% of S&P's drawdown).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Sharpe is structurally lower (0.2-0.5 normal) because both excess return and volatility are compressed. For passive Treasury / IG funds, Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was an efficient exposure — do not Fail a passive fund for matching its index's Sharpe. Drawdown in 2022 should match the duration-matched Treasury index.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Sharpe is usually 0.3-0.6 mid-cycle and can go deeply negative in credit shock years (2008, 2020 partial). Multi-year Sharpe matters more than single-year; compare to the matching credit benchmark's Sharpe in the same window. Drawdown test: HY drew -22% in 2008, -15-20% in 2020 — in-line with the credit index is Pass; materially worse suggests excess exposure to lower-rated or concentrated issuers.

factors.factorTitle: How This Fund Handles Risk vs Its Category Peers
factors.factorDescription: How does this fund's risk profile and risk-management discipline compare to its same-category peers across 3Y / 5Y / 10Y? Uses category-relative risk score, peer-relative risk level, and the four-outcome test: above-average risk WITH above-average return = acceptable trade; above-average risk WITHOUT above-average return = clear Fail; below-average risk with similar-or-better return = strong risk discipline; below-average risk with weaker return = trading return for safety (fine for conservative sleeves). This factor also carries the within-category structural risk lens — for sector / thematic funds the concentration-risk check (top-10 weight, single-name max, sub-sector / country concentration); for credit-and-income funds the credit-quality-mix check vs category (reaching for yield by holding lower-rated issuers should show up here); for passive funds the structural fee + tracking-cost headwind in active-heavy peer groups means median risk-vs-category is a Pass-grade outcome. Always report peer-group size alongside the rank — median in a 600-fund category is very different from median in a 10-fund category. Pass when risk is at or below the category median, OR the extra risk is clearly compensated by better category-relative returns over the longest available window, OR the fund is a passive tracking its index inside an active-heavy peer set. Fail when risk sits consistently above the category median across multiple periods without above-average returns to justify it, OR when within-category structural risk (single-name concentration above ~15%, credit-mix outside category norms) is meaningful and unflagged by the marketing label.
factors.factorMetrics: riskScore, riskLevel, riskVsCategory, returnVsCategory, riskPeriods, percentileRanks, quartileRanks, numberOfInvestmentsInCategory, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Risk-to-category should track the index for passive funds; do not Fail a passive Large Blend for category-like risk — that is the index moving. For style tilts (value, growth, low-vol, momentum, quality), check whether the tilt's risk profile matches its claim — a 'low-volatility' fund whose category risk reads at-or-above median is failing its mandate.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Concentration risk is the within-category red flag here: check top-10 holdings weight (below ~40% diversified, 40-60% typical, above 60% means fund fate tied to a handful of names), single-name maximum (anything above 10% is meaningful single-stock risk — several semi funds carry ~15% NVDA), and sub-sector / single-country concentration within a theme. Compare drawdown depth in sector down-cycles to sector peers.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Risk-vs-category is mostly daily-tracking quality across the leveraged peer set — does this 3x track better than the other 3x in the category? Structural decay applies to every product in the category, so do not Fail on rank alone if the decay is in line with peers. Watch for tracking divergence in stressed underlyings (the inverse oil and inverse-volatility blowups of 2018 / 2020).
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Small peer counts (Commodities Broad Basket, Commodities Precious Metals, Digital Assets, Single Currency) — report peer count alongside any rank. Physical-backed vs futures-based wrappers within the same category have different risk profiles (futures-based carry roll-cost drag); note which sub-group the rank is against. For crypto, custody structure and issuer track-record matter alongside the headline risk metrics.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Within Derivative Income, Defined Outcome, Equity Hedged, Multistrategy, Systematic Trend, Long-Short Equity, Market Neutral, Macro Trading, Multialternative, Event Driven, Relative Value Arbitrage, Volatility, Downside Hedge — these are separate categories with different risk norms. Within Derivative Income especially, dispersion is wide (JEPI vs QYLD vs SPYI have different option mechanics, underlying indices, and ROC composition). Compare to the right sub-category.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare within the specific dated vintage (Target-Date 2030, 2040, 2050…) or allocation bucket (Conservative / Moderate / Aggressive / Tactical / Global Allocation / Target Risk / Target Outcome). Risk vs category should reflect the stated bucket; a 'moderate' fund whose risk sits with the 'aggressive' peer group is mis-bucketed and should be flagged.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Duration-bucketed peer comparison (Ultrashort, Short-Term, Intermediate Core, Long-Term, Long Government, Inflation-Protected). Compare credit-quality mix to peers — a 'core' fund holding 30%+ BBB or non-IG splinters is reaching for yield and should show up as above-average risk vs its core peers. For muni, check the AMT exposure / private-activity-bond share alongside the duration.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-tier peer comparison (High Yield Bond, Bank Loan, Preferred Stock, Convertibles, Emerging Markets Bond, Multisector Bond, Nontraditional Bond, High Yield Muni, Securitized Bond-Focused). Funds reaching for yield by holding lower-rated issuers should show up as above-average risk that better be paid for in returns. For preferreds and CLOs, tranche / subordination position matters alongside the headline credit grade.

factors.factorTitle: Macro Risk — Economy, Industry Cycle, Rates, Currency
factors.factorDescription: How sensitive is the fund to macro forces — broad economic cycles, sector / industry cycles, interest-rate path, currency moves, and global macro shocks? Different funds carry different macro exposures: equity funds carry economic-cycle risk; bond funds carry interest-rate risk magnified by duration; international funds add currency risk; sector funds carry industry-cycle risk; commodity funds carry commodity-cycle and geopolitical risk; crypto funds carry regulatory and adoption-cycle risk; allocation funds inherit the macro risk of their underlying sleeves. The retail question: 'What macro environments would hurt this fund, and how severely?' Use the fund's behavior in past macro shocks (2008 GFC recession, 2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2018 trade war, 2014-2016 oil crash) as the empirical test. For young funds without that history, judge by stated exposures and category analogues. Pass when the fund's macro sensitivity is consistent with its mandate and category — a long-duration bond fund that lost 25-30% in 2022 was doing what duration does; a global allocation fund that gave up 5pp to USD strength in 2022 was bearing currency risk inherent to the mandate. Fail when macro exposure is materially larger than the category norm without being disclosed, OR when the fund makes an unannounced macro bet (large duration, large country tilt, large sector concentration) that retail holders cannot easily see.
factors.factorMetrics: duration, beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, riskPeriods, returnsAnnual

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Economic-cycle risk is the dominant macro factor — recessions drop broad equity -20% to -35%. Foreign and global funds add currency risk: a USD-strengthening year like 2022 cost foreign-equity returns to USD investors materially. Fed-cycle matters too — growth-tilted funds suffer more in rising-rate cycles than value-tilted ones; high-dividend funds behave like a duration substitute when rates fall.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Industry-cycle risk is the primary macro factor — tech sensitive to rates and capex cycles; energy to oil prices and OPEC+ decisions; financials to the yield curve; REITs to rates; biotech to FDA and reimbursement policy; utilities and consumer staples are defensive but rate-sensitive. Single-country EM adds currency, political, and capital-controls risk (China's tech regulatory crackdown 2021-22, India's rupee moves, Latin American currency shocks).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Macro shocks are amplified by the leverage factor. A 3x long-equity fund in a Fed-tightening cycle is a leveraged bet that no recession lands soon; a 3x long-bond fund is a leveraged duration bet. State the macro position retail is implicitly taking. Inverse funds in trending environments compound favorably; in choppy environments they bleed regardless of direction.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Commodity-cycle risk (oil supply / OPEC+, agricultural seasonality, mining production, industrial demand); USD strength inversely correlates with commodity prices; geopolitical events drive sharp moves (Middle East, Russia / Ukraine, OPEC+ decisions, sanctions). Crypto carries regulatory risk (SEC enforcement actions, country-level bans, stablecoin rules) and adoption-cycle risk; post-2022 BTC has correlated with risk-on / risk-off equities more than its 'digital gold' marketing suggests.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Sensitive to the volatility regime — option premium is small in low-vol regimes (covered-call yield shrinks), large in high-vol regimes. Long-short and managed-futures depend on trend / dispersion environments; market-neutral suffers in crowded-quant unwinds. Buffer / defined-outcome funds carry interest-rate risk through their option-pricing and reference-rate components. Macro stress test: 2008 GFC, 2018 vol spike, 2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock — how did each strategy hold up?
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Macro risk is the blend of equity, bond, and (sometimes) alt sleeves. Rising-rate environments hurt the bond sleeve materially — 2022 cost moderate-allocation funds -16% even though equity-only was -25%, because the typical 40% bond sleeve lost ~-13%. Currency exposure for global allocation funds adds another layer. Tactical funds carry manager-call risk on top of asset-class risk.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Interest-rate risk is the single dominant macro: duration × rate move = expected price loss. Long-duration government / muni (15Y+) lost -25% to -31% in 2022; intermediate core (5-7Y) -10% to -15%; ultrashort (<3Y) a few percent; MMF essentially flat. Match the fund's duration to the investor's holding horizon and rate view; long-duration is a directional rate bet. Foreign IG adds currency considerations (hedged vs unhedged matters materially).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-cycle risk is the primary macro — recessions widen spreads and trigger downgrades / defaults (2008 GFC: HY -22%; 2020 COVID: HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%). Rate sensitivity is secondary for longer-dated members (preferred 5-6Y duration, EM debt 6-8Y, convertibles often closer to equity). EM debt layers currency, sovereign, and geopolitical risk. Bank loans and AAA CLOs are partly insulated from rates (floating-rate coupons reset).

factors.factorDescription: Does the fund have a structural mechanism that erodes NAV over time, separate from market drawdowns? Three classic structural erosion mechanics, each tied to a specific group: (1) daily-reset compounding decay in leveraged / inverse products; (2) return-of-capital propping distributions in covered-call wrappers and some preferred / convertible / EM-debt funds; (3) futures-roll cost (contango drag) in futures-based commodity wrappers (USO famously lost ~94% of NAV since inception from roll cost). For the groups where one of these mechanics is present, the factor also asks the strategy-validation question: does the strategy deliver enough return / income / utility to justify the structural cost? A leveraged fund whose realized CAGR is well below leverage × underlying's CAGR and which is also being marketed for buy-and-hold is failing both tests. A covered-call fund whose 10Y NAV has materially declined while distributions kept paying — and where ROC is a large share — is paying retail with their own money. A futures-based commodity wrapper whose 5Y price return is far below the spot underlying is the contango story; the question is whether the diversification or directional-exposure benefit makes it worth the drag. For groups where no structural NAV-erosion mechanic exists (broad-equity, sector-thematic-equity, allocation-target-date, fixed-income-investment-grade, most of fixed-income-credit-and-income), this section is short — note 'no structural NAV-erosion risk in this group' and move on; the related concern there is fee-drag, not structural erosion. Pass when there is no structural mechanic, OR the strategy is delivering enough to justify the structural cost. Fail when structural NAV erosion is present AND the strategy is not delivering offsetting value (10Y flat-to-negative NAV with marginal total return in a covered-call fund; daily-tracking breakdown plus buy-and-hold marketing in a leveraged product; futures-commodity fund whose roll cost has consumed multi-year price returns without delivering the diversification it claimed).
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, change1y, change5y, change10y, returnsAnnual, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, dividendTtm, divGrowth3y, divGrowth5y, beta, beta1y, drawdown

groupInstructions.broad-equity: No structural NAV-erosion mechanic in plain broad-equity. Keep this section brief: the only related concern is fee-drag — a high-fee or active broad-equity fund where the expense ratio compounds into a multi-year cumulative shortfall vs the index. State 'no structural NAV erosion in this group' and call out fee drag only if it is material.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: No structural NAV-erosion mechanic per se. Related concern: thematic-fund liquidation risk — when AUM drops below survival threshold and the issuer closes or merges the fund, retail holders may be forced out at the bottom of the cycle. Cross-reference the AUM Growth Trend factor's read here. State 'no structural NAV erosion' if AUM is healthy.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: NAV erosion is structural and central — daily-reset path dependency. Quote the underlying's CAGR × the stated leverage factor as the textbook expectation, show the realized result, and the gap IS the structural NAV erosion. Strategy-validation test: leveraged products are useful for short-term directional trading — has the fund maintained tight daily-tracking quality? If yes and the product is correctly marketed as short-term, the structural decay is the trade-off, not a Fail. Fail when daily tracking has broken down OR the product is being marketed for buy-and-hold.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Structural NAV erosion applies to the futures-based subset (USO, UNG, some broad-commodity futures wrappers) via contango / roll cost — USO lost ~94% of NAV since inception. Physical-backed gold / silver and spot-crypto wrappers (GLD, IAU, IBIT, FBTC) do not have this mechanic — note explicitly which sub-type the fund belongs to. For futures wrappers, the strategy-validation test is whether the diversification or directional-exposure benefit is worth the structural drag; for physical and spot wrappers, the section is short.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: NAV erosion via return-of-capital is the central structural risk here. Break out the ROC share of distributions in the latest year (often 40%+ for QYLD-style funds, lower for JEPI). Show the 5Y / 10Y NAV path alongside cumulative distributions — a flat-to-positive total return on top of a steadily declining NAV is 'paying you with your own money'. Strategy-validation test: covered-call funds should deliver yield + capped upside + cushion in down markets; if all three are present and ROC is moderate (under ~30%), the strategy is justifying its structural cost. Fail when ROC dominates AND the underlying long-term price has materially declined; QYLD vs JEPI is the textbook contrast.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: No structural NAV-erosion mechanic in standard allocation / target-date funds — distributions come from real coupon and dividend income on the underlying sleeves. Exception: 'managed payout' or 'through-retirement' variants that pay above the natural sleeve yield can include ROC; flag those specifically. Otherwise state 'no structural NAV erosion' and keep this section brief.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: No structural NAV erosion in standard IG bond funds — NAV moves with rates and mean-reverts as new coupons reset at higher yields; distribution funded by real coupon income. State 'no structural NAV erosion' and keep this section brief. Related concern: a TIPS or muni fund quoting a TTM yield that is materially higher than its SEC yield is smoothing, not eroding — note the divergence if present.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Mostly no structural erosion in plain HY / bank-loan funds (real coupon income, distribution tracks SEC yield). Exception: some preferred / convertible / EM-debt wrappers where ROC can be material — break out the ROC share where present. Strategy-validation test: credit funds are taking real default and subordination risk for higher yield; the multi-year total return vs the IG corporate alternative is the validation — if HY underperformed IG over a 5Y / 10Y window, the credit risk was not paid for.
